nodes.name: Photography & Modern Art
nodes.description: Landmark photographers and contemporary artists from the 20th and 21st centuries

items.prompt: Which American photographer, famous for dramatic black-and-white images of the American West (especially Yosemite), co-developed the Zone System for precise exposure control and co-founded Group f/64?
items.explanation:
American, 1902–1984, born in San Francisco
Best known for majestic black-and-white landscape photographs of Yosemite, the Sierra Nevada, and the American Southwest
First visited Yosemite at age 14; received his first camera (a Kodak Brownie) on that trip
Co-developed the Zone System with Fred Archer in the 1930s — a method of controlling exposure and development to precisely map tonal values from pure black (Zone 0) to pure white (Zone X)
Co-founded Group f/64 in 1932 with Edward Weston and others, advocating sharp-focus 'straight photography' over soft-focus pictorialism
A passionate environmentalist; his photographs helped persuade Congress to establish Kings Canyon National Park (1940)
His *Moonrise, Hernandez, New Mexico* (1941) is one of the most valuable photographs ever — a print sold for $609,600 in 2006
Received the Presidential Medal of Freedom in 1980
items.cardFront: Landscape photography — Yosemite, Zone System, black-and-white

items.prompt: Which 1962 artwork consisting of 32 hand-painted canvases, each depicting a different variety of a canned food product, launched Pop Art into mainstream attention when first exhibited at the Ferus Gallery in Los Angeles?
alternates: Campbell's Soup Cans by Andy Warhol
items.explanation:
Andy Warhol, synthetic polymer paint on canvas (32 canvases), 1962, Museum of Modern Art (MoMA), New York
Each of the 32 canvases depicts a different variety of Campbell's condensed soup — one for every flavor the company sold at the time
First exhibited at the Ferus Gallery in Los Angeles in July 1962, displayed on shelves like products in a grocery store
Warhol said he chose soup because he had it for lunch every day for 20 years — though this may be apocryphal
Each canvas measures 50.8 x 40.6 cm (20 x 16 in); the earliest canvases were hand-painted, not silkscreened
The work challenged the boundary between fine art and commercial design, a central Pop Art concern
MoMA acquired all 32 canvases as a set in 1996 for approximately $15 million
Campbell's initially considered legal action but eventually embraced the association, commissioning Warhol to paint a can of their new tomato-rice flavor
items.cardFront: Pop Art — 32 canned soup paintings, 1962, Ferus Gallery debut

items.prompt: Which 1917 'readymade' — a mass-produced porcelain urinal turned on its side and signed 'R. Mutt' — was submitted to a New York exhibition by a French-American artist and is considered the most influential artwork of the 20th century?
items.explanation:
Marcel Duchamp, porcelain urinal (readymade), 1917 (original lost; replicas from 1950 onward)
A standard Bedfordshire-type urinal purchased from J.L. Mott Iron Works in New York, turned 90 degrees and signed 'R. Mutt 1917'
Submitted to the Society of Independent Artists exhibition in 1917, which claimed to accept all works; the committee rejected it regardless
Duchamp resigned from the Society's board in protest; the rejection became the point — who decides what is art?
The original was lost (likely discarded); Duchamp authorized 17 replicas in the 1950s and 1960s
Voted the most influential artwork of the 20th century in a 2004 survey of 500 art professionals by Turner Prize sponsors
Coined the concept of the 'readymade' — an ordinary manufactured object designated as art by the artist's choice
Duchamp's other readymades include a bicycle wheel on a stool (1913) and a snow shovel titled *In Advance of the Broken Arm* (1915)
items.cardFront: Conceptual art — urinal readymade signed 'R. Mutt,' 1917

items.prompt: Which British artist, a leading member of the Young British Artists group, placed a 14-foot tiger shark in formaldehyde in a glass case in 1991, titling it *The Physical Impossibility of Death in the Mind of Someone Living*?
items.explanation:
British, born 1965 in Bristol, raised in Leeds
The shark in formaldehyde, *The Physical Impossibility of Death in the Mind of Someone Living* (1991), was commissioned by collector Charles Saatchi for £50,000
The original shark deteriorated and was replaced with a new specimen in 2006
A leading member of the Young British Artists (YBAs) who emerged from Goldsmiths College in the late 1980s
His diamond-encrusted platinum skull, *For the Love of God* (2007), reportedly cost £14 million to produce and was listed at £50 million
Organized the landmark *Freeze* exhibition (1988) while still a student, which launched the YBA movement
*Beautiful Inside My Head Forever* (2008) was sold directly at Sotheby's, bypassing galleries, and raised £111 million in two days
His 'spot paintings' and 'spin paintings' are deliberately produced by studio assistants, challenging notions of artistic authorship
items.cardFront: YBA — shark in formaldehyde, diamond skull

items.prompt: Which American artist is known for monumental stainless-steel sculptures of everyday objects like balloon animals, exhibited at venues including the Palace of Versailles, and whose *Rabbit* (1986) sold for $91.1 million in 2019?
items.explanation:
American, born 1955 in York, Pennsylvania
*Balloon Dog* sculptures are his most recognizable works — five unique versions in different colors, each over 10 feet tall, made of mirror-polished stainless steel
*Rabbit* (1986), a stainless-steel cast of an inflatable rabbit, sold at Christie's in 2019 for $91.1 million — a record for a living artist at the time
Works are manufactured by teams of over 100 studio assistants using industrial processes; Koons directs but rarely fabricates
Elevates kitsch and commercial objects to high art: vacuum cleaners in display cases, porcelain Michael Jackson, giant Play-Doh pile
A retrospective at the Palace of Versailles in 2008 was both celebrated and controversial
Worked as a Wall Street commodities broker in the early 1980s to fund his art
Critics are sharply divided: some see brilliant commentary on consumer culture, others see cynical commercialism

items.prompt: Which Chinese dissident artist and activist created an installation of 100 million hand-painted porcelain sunflower seeds at Tate Modern in 2010, and helped design the 'Bird's Nest' stadium for the 2008 Beijing Olympics before publicly disavowing the games?
items.explanation:
Chinese, born 1957 in Beijing; son of poet Ai Qing, who was persecuted during the Cultural Revolution
*Sunflower Seeds* (2010) at Tate Modern: 100 million hand-crafted porcelain seeds, each individually sculpted and painted by 1,600 workers in Jingdezhen over two and a half years
Collaborated with architects Herzog & de Meuron on the Beijing National Stadium ('Bird's Nest') for the 2008 Olympics, then publicly boycotted the games as propaganda
Detained by Chinese authorities for 81 days in 2011 without formal charges; his passport was confiscated for four years
*Remembering* (2009): covered the facade of Munich's Haus der Kunst with 9,000 children's backpacks spelling out a quote about the Sichuan earthquake, in which thousands of children died in poorly built schools
Documented the European refugee crisis in the film *Human Flow* (2017)
His Beijing studio was demolished by Chinese authorities in 2011; now lives in exile, primarily in Portugal
Uses social media (especially Instagram) as an artistic and activist medium
items.cardFront: Chinese dissident — *Sunflower Seeds*, Bird's Nest collaborator

items.prompt: Which Japanese artist, born in 1929 and voluntarily living in a psychiatric institution since 1977, is known for obsessive polka-dot patterns and immersive mirrored rooms that create the illusion of infinite space?
items.explanation:
Japanese, born 1929 in Matsumoto City, Nagano Prefecture; has voluntarily lived in a psychiatric hospital in Tokyo since 1977
Infinity Mirror Rooms: immersive installations using mirrors and lights to create the sensation of endless, cosmic space; first created in 1965
Her obsessive repetition of polka dots stems from childhood hallucinations she describes as seeing dots covering everything
Moved to New York in 1958; became part of the avant-garde scene alongside Donald Judd and Claes Oldenburg
Organized nude happenings and anti-war protests in New York in the 1960s; returned to Japan in 1973
Her pumpkin sculptures, often covered in polka dots, have become iconic — a yellow pumpkin on Naoshima island is a major tourist attraction
Now the world's top-selling female artist at auction; her works regularly sell for tens of millions
Produces paintings daily from her studio near the hospital, often on large canvases covered in her signature 'infinity nets'
items.cardFront: Japanese — Infinity Mirror Rooms, polka dots, pumpkins

items.prompt: Which American artist created *Untitled Film Stills* (1977–1980), a series of 69 black-and-white photographs in which she posed as stereotypical female characters from 1950s and 1960s B-movies, exploring identity and gender representation?
items.explanation:
American, born 1954 in Glen Ridge, New Jersey
*Untitled Film Stills* (1977–1980): 69 black-and-white photographs in which she appears as various female archetypes inspired by 1950s–1960s film and media imagery
Every photograph features Sherman herself, but she rejects being called a self-portrait artist — the work is about constructed identity, not autobiography
The complete set of *Untitled Film Stills* was acquired by MoMA in 1995 for $1 million
Her *Untitled #96* (1981) — a centerfold-format image of a teenage girl clutching a newspaper clipping — sold for $3.89 million in 2011
Later series explored grotesque and disturbing imagery: prosthetic body parts, clown faces, fairy tales, Old Master paintings
Part of the Pictures Generation of artists (alongside Richard Prince and Sherrie Levine) who deconstructed mass media imagery
Received a MacArthur Fellowship in 1995

items.prompt: Which American-born artist in Paris invented 'Rayographs' — cameraless photographs made by placing objects directly on light-sensitive paper — and was a key figure in both the Dada and Surrealist movements?
items.explanation:
American, 1890–1976, born Emmanuel Radnitzky in Philadelphia; lived in Paris for most of his career
Invented the 'Rayograph' (his term for photograms): images made without a camera by placing objects directly on photosensitized paper and exposing it to light
Published *Les Champs délicieux* (1922), a book of Rayographs, with an introduction by Dada leader Tristan Tzara
Rediscovered solarization (the Sabattier effect) with his assistant and lover Lee Miller, creating an eerie halo around photographic subjects
A central figure in both Dada and Surrealism; close associates included Marcel Duchamp, André Breton, and Salvador Dalí
Also a painter, sculptor, and filmmaker; directed the experimental film *Le Retour à la raison* (1923)
His fashion and portrait photography appeared in *Vogue* and *Harper's Bazaar*, giving Surrealist aesthetics a commercial audience
Died in Paris in 1976; his epitaph reads 'unconcerned, but not indifferent'

items.prompt: Which American photographer, known for austere black-and-white images of flowers, celebrity portraits, and explicitly homoerotic male nudes, sparked a national debate over government arts funding when a 1990 exhibition in Cincinnati led to obscenity charges?
items.explanation:
American, 1946–1989, born in Floral Park, Queens, New York
Known for technically precise black-and-white photographs: flowers (especially calla lilies and orchids), celebrity portraits, and male nudes
His explicitly homoerotic and sadomasochistic images ignited the 1989–1990 'culture wars' over government arts funding in the United States
An NEA-funded retrospective, *The Perfect Moment*, led to the Contemporary Arts Center in Cincinnati being charged with obscenity in 1990; the director was acquitted
The controversy prompted Congress to impose content restrictions on National Endowment for the Arts grants
Close companion of Patti Smith for years; she documented their relationship in the memoir *Just Kids* (2010), which won the National Book Award
Died of AIDS-related complications on March 9, 1989, at age 42
Established the Robert Mapplethorpe Foundation before his death to fund photography exhibitions and AIDS research
items.cardFront: Photography — flowers, male nudes, NEA funding controversy

items.prompt: Which American documentary photographer captured *Migrant Mother* in 1936 — a portrait of Florence Owens Thompson and her children at a California pea-pickers camp that became the defining image of the Great Depression?
items.explanation:
American, 1895–1965, born in Hoboken, New Jersey
*Migrant Mother* (March 1936) depicts Florence Owens Thompson, age 32, with three of her seven children at a pea-pickers camp in Nipomo, California
Lange made six exposures, moving progressively closer; the final image became the most iconic photograph of the Great Depression
The photograph was published in newspapers and prompted the federal government to send 20,000 pounds of food to the camp
Employed by the Farm Security Administration (FSA) to document rural poverty and migrant workers across America
Also photographed the internment of Japanese Americans during WWII — the Army impounded many of her images as too sympathetic
Thompson's identity was not publicly known until 1978 when a reporter tracked her down
Lange died in 1965, shortly before a major retrospective of her work opened at MoMA

items.prompt: Which artistic duo, married partners born on the same day in 1935, wrapped the Reichstag in Berlin in silvery fabric in 1995, surrounded islands in Miami with pink fabric, and created massive outdoor installations that existed only temporarily?
items.explanation:
Christo Vladimirov Javacheff (Bulgarian-born) and Jeanne-Claude Denat de Guillebon (French-born), both born June 13, 1935
Wrapped Reichstag, Berlin (1995): 100,000 square meters of silvery fabric and 15 km of blue rope covering the German parliament building for two weeks; seen by 5 million visitors
*Surrounded Islands* (1983): 11 islands in Miami's Biscayne Bay encircled with 603,850 square meters of pink polypropylene fabric
*The Gates* (2005): 7,503 saffron-colored fabric panels lining 37 km of walkways in New York's Central Park for 16 days
All installations were temporary by design — the transient nature was central to the art
Refused all sponsorship and government funding; financed every project through the sale of preparatory drawings, models, and collages
Jeanne-Claude died in 2009; Christo continued working and died in 2020
Their final project, *L'Arc de Triomphe, Wrapped*, was realized posthumously in Paris in September 2021
items.cardFront: Environmental installations — Wrapped Reichstag, *The Gates*, temporary art
